speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Scott, tell about the events leading up to your working at the Belgian Chamber of Commerce. How did you start working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I met Simone. [CLEARS THROAT] She was introduced to me. And she asked me if I would come and be her secretary. And I did. I was very happy to accept the job. And I was very happy to work with her. She was a very intelligent, very bright girl-- 25 years old. She spoke, I think, two or three languages herself. And she was a wonderful person to work with.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her position at the Chamber of Commerce?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was director of the Chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of work did you start doing for her initially?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, interviewing people when they came to look for work, or they came and asked us information-- like they do in all Chamber of Commerce, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a very busy place to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, very. Very busy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of people do remember coming and going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Businesspeople, businessmen, business ladies, very few young people. I mean, not young people at 16-- you know, people over 20 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Alice doing at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Alice, your daughter, doing at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chamber-- but she was still going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were living with your mother and father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My dad died suddenly in 1940. I think he got-- I think he became very frightened of the idea of war. And he had a little heart problem. He died very suddenly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately how long did you work at the Belgian Chamber of Commerce before you became aware that Simone was involved in underground activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very long-- maybe two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me how you first became aware of that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I told you, I wanted to go to Belgium. I needed a passport. So she told me, don't worry about that. I'll get a passport. And then I went to Brussels, saw my lawyer. [CLEARS THROAT] I came back. Everything was fine. And then shortly after that, I don't know how long-- not long. She said-- I said I had to go back to see my lawyer again. And then I was in contact with the gentleman, my boss, for whom I was working. So she said, well that's OK. Your passport, it's still good. But I don't-- I had a funny feeling every time I took the train, that I was being watched, more or less. I don't know by individuals, but German people, German men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, one day--

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: --she told me, listen. You're going to Brussels. Do you mind bringing some papers at a certain place? I said, no. I don't mind. Why not? So I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CLEARS THROAT] It was at the Unknown Soldier, in Brussels. And I went to bring some papers there. And I felt somebody was following me. And I brought the papers there. And when I came out, I didn't see that individual. But I did see him a little bit later on as I was walking in the street. He was following me. And I said, that's funny, the way I'm being followed. And anyway, I lost him. I went in the stores, you know, in a big department store. I wasn't thinking really, at that time. And when I came back to Paris, I told Simone. And then she told me, she said, it's not fair. I didn't tell you what it was. But here's what it is. And she explained to me that she was working for the underground. And she said, I don't want-- you have a daughter. And you have your family. She had her family too. She said, I don't want to get you involved. But she said, if you want to, if you're interested-- and I said sure. Go ahead. And I started like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you angry with her for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Angry? No. No. I was a little deceived. But I was not angry.

speaker: interviewer
text: When she asked you to join the underground, did you make--

speaker: interviewee
text: She asked me if I wanted to, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make your decision to join right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't need to think about it? Tell me why it was such an easy decision for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because I had started. And I said, well I've started now. It's that easy. I'm going there anyway. So. But I didn't really-- to tell you the truth, I never thought I'd be caught. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: I never thought they'd catch me. When you first joined the underground, what type of work did you think you would be doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, I carried all kinds of documents in fur coats. I'd wear a fur coat, and I had documents in them. I had bottles of oil. You couldn't get salad oil during the war. But she would get it. And they would double-- I can't say it in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: A false bottom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And there were things in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you acted as a courier when you first began?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would you take all the papers and whatever it is that you had to carry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where would I take them?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: All different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about a typical job that you would have. Tell me about a particular day when you had to go take something to someone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Particular day? A day? I couldn't tell you what day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me one incident. You don't have to tell me the day, but tell me a specific incident when you had to take some papers to someone. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: You want a day? The day?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. I don't want the day. I want you tell me about one day when you took some papers, and you had to deliver them to somebody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I just told you there, about the one that was following me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened another time. I was in the train. And I had a fur coat on. And I had documents in my coat. And my coat kept slipping off my shoulders. And there was an individual next to me. I knew he was German. But he kept wanting to help me. And he spoke very broken English. And I said, no. I said, I'm fine this way. I said, if I take it off, you never could tell. He could feel I had something under my coat. That's the only time really, that I felt that he was really next to me, in back of me. Otherwise, I wasn't afraid, but I felt uncomfortable, very uncomfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in underground work two days out of the week? Three days out of the week?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Made false IDs. That's how we saved the Jewish people, by making false IDs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at the office, at the Chamber of Commerce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you made the false IDs.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were cards, the little cards, like an ID card. But we put their names on it, put their birth, where they came from, just like you do here. And Simone had a machine. She stamped it. And then the papers-- we made out papers. And we sent them in certain places, in the south of France, most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the names of the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us it. They came to the office. They knew that the Chamber of Commerce-- how they knew it, I don't know. There were all kinds of underground things that gave them the-- where they had to go. So they had the excuse that they wanted to find work, or they wanted this. And they'd come to us. And when they came to us, we-- they had a certain-- how do they say-- a certain ID on them, or a certain sign on them, that we knew what they wanted. Simone had arranged all that. She did everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do any of the arranging at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it only Simone that did the arranging?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. Nobody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would come in with a photograph, and you would make the ID?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They had a photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the equipment in the Chamber of Commerce?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the office, right in the office-- well, in the back. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone else at the Chamber of Commerce that was aware of what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nope. Nobody. Everything was very well arranged.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in any given week, approximately how many passports would you-- would you make?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes we did 25 a day-- not every day, but I would say at least maybe 200, 300 a week. These poor people were trying to get away, get away-- so many-- and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long would it take you to make one passport?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 minutes-- had to go fast. But the doors were closed, in any case. There was-- [CLEARS THROAT] --somebody that watched at the door that-- they didn't know what for, but they didn't let anyone-- it was during the war, so they didn't let anybody in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do other than make passports?

speaker: interviewee
text: In between?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Worked for the people that came and asked information-- the Chamber of Commerce-- for work, or information for travel, anything that you ask in a Chamber of Commerce. I don't know what they ask here. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you go and ask here? Were your parents aware of what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody. My parents didn't know where I was for 3 and 1/2 years. They didn't know I'd been arrested or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were arrested, they weren't aware of what you were doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone--

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew I worked at the Chamber of Commerce, that's all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you travel to Brussels very often during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, about I'd say two or three times a month, in the month, maybe during two months. Then I'd stop for a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long to take the train from Paris to Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: About four hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would you often go there just for a day and then come back in the evening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. But sometimes I stayed longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would you stay in Brussels?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: I told you. I gave you the address.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell--

speaker: interviewee
text: [FRENCH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was that apartment used by other people as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was only mine, under my name. That's where they came and arrested me-- the Gestapo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me backtrack a little bit. Did you have any contact with anyone in the underground other than Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I had Lillian, and I had the other girls, that I showed you pictures. But contact without-- we had contact without having contact. We'd meet each other like-- oh. I'd-- I'd be walking the street. We knew we're going to meet each other. We didn't-- make believe that we knew each other. You know what I mean? I can't explain myself. We had contact, but we never went out and had dinner together, or things like that. Not in groups-- never.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a woman named Lillian Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: You mention someone named Lillian. Lillian who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian? Yeah, Lillian, the parachutist.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's Lillian's last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Freter, F-R-E-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about Lillian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian was a person married to a doctor. She was from a very good family. She was from a noble family in England. And her mother was a-- not a noble lady, but a very-- she was a noble lady, but not noble background, but a very, very nice lady. And they had quite a lot of money. Her brother was the hairdresser at the King's Palace. And he had his own-- how would I say-- salon de coiffure. Not-- I wouldn't call that a beauty-- beauty place, but a place where you could go and have your hair done, and your and nails, and all that, but something really, really very chic, in the best quarters of Brussels-- very rich. And she was an only child. For what-- the little-- I knew her long time, but I didn't know too much of her family background. But I know that she had a daughter. She had been married. She had a daughter that was about-- when I knew her, about-- she must have been about 14-- about Jenny's age-- that I met after. She was married to a-- I think he was from the Flanders. He was from a very good family from the Flan-- but anyway. They didn't get along. And she divorced. And she had the daughter. That's as much as I know of her family. I know she became a very good friend of mine. And after the war, we stayed very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the work that you did with her during the war in the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the work you and Lillian did together in the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't even see-- she wasn't with me. We worked for the underground together, but she wasn't where I was. As a parachuter, they sent her everywhere. She was in the north of France. She was in the south of France. Anywhere where the Germans could be, they sent her. And she spoke several languages too. And then as I told you, one day, she was coming down in the north of France, in a parachute. And a German shot at her. And the bullet went in back of her heart. And in her back, came out under her heart. And she killed him. And then the-- the farmers-- it was in the country-- took it. Thank you. The farmers came and rescued her. And they took care for her. They sent her probably-- I don't know where-- where she was. But anyway, I met her when I was in Ravensbruck. Somebody came. And they called me. And they said, Mick, Mick! Somebody you know! Somebody you know! Come and see! It's your daughter! Oh my heaven. My heart stopped beating, my daughter. She was still young. I said, no it can't be. It was Lillian. From that day on we never, never left each other, never ever. We decided if they did something to us, that we would die together. And I remember the day of our-- well you don't want to get there yet-- liberation. I'll tell you that after then.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned a number of other girls that you worked with.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other girls?

speaker: interviewer
text: In the underground.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I didn't know their names. We didn't know our names. We just knew we had a number that we'd call each other. But we didn't know our names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have special code words that you'd use when you would meet them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I tried to stay out of being in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't like doing the courier work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I liked it. It was exciting-- [LAUGHS] --until I knew I'd gone too far. But I couldn't get back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it exciting?

speaker: interviewee
text: When you do something you know you're not supposed to do, but you want to do it. Because you want it to get there, you know? But I never thought I'd be caught. So I thought it was interesting. Even the gentleman I was going to marry didn't know it. And I used to go each time I went to Brussels. I would see him, and have dinner, and go out with him, and all up, and I never told him anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jean. John. Depauw, D-E-P-A-U-W, Depauw.

speaker: interviewer
text: When Simone first asked you to join the underground, did she tell you about the dangers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not really. She just said, you know what? You're exposed to-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your response?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, OK. I know. But I told her I have to go anyway, so what difference? I have to go to Brussels anyway. That's the way I took it. I thought, I'm going, I might as well do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you join the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why. I can't tell you why. Because I thought, just as I told you. I was going anyway to Brussels. So I thought I might as well do something if it's going to help our country, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel you were helping the Jews and the country? Both?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know, because I knew-- I didn't know her name, but I know a young girl. She was just a very ordinary young French girl. She was 16 years old. I met her in the camp after we were working at Siemens factory. She blew up the whole theater, Gaumont Theater, in Paris, by herself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I met her in the camp when we were working at Siemens. We called her Dolly, but that was not her name. She must-- I don't know if she lived or what, because when we were liberated, she wasn't liberated the same time I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit more about the passports that you were making. Were you using false names? Or what would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, false names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where would you get the names from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Any name they wanted to give us. They'd give us a false name. They'd give a false name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Jewish person would give you the false--

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I call myself Micky. Well, they call themselves different names. Don't ask me their names, because I don't know the Jewish names.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just knew the name they gave you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. Well, that was up to them to give us the names. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most of them were foreign names anyway. Lots of them-- lots of them were German Jewish people that were running away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of person was Simone?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of person? What do you mean? Physically?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did she look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wasn't very tall, a little bit taller than me. [LAUGHS] I know I'm short. She was a little bit taller than me, same as on her picture, very pretty face. She had beautiful eyes, big blue eyes, well built, and always laughing, always, always in a good mood, and always singing. She'd make a passport-- she was still singing. She had a beautiful voice. And I don't know why she would always sing 'Ave Maria.' Oh she loved that. And she'd sing it so well. And I would tell her. Oh, Simone, sing that for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- well-- you're not to the liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she sing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she sing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did she sing it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: What do mean how? I can't-- she had a beautiful voice. And she knew it. And she had learned singing, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever sing? Or were you just a dancer? Did you sing while you danced?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I haven't got a good voice. My mother did. My mother was an opera singer. And my sister, Yvonne, both sang very, very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was almost as if she wasn't afraid of what she was doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Simone? No. Never. Never afraid. But she knew what she was doing. When I started, I didn't know. But she knew. But I was never angry at her for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever remember her being afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. But she suffered. Oh. There, probably, she was afraid, poor girl. When they caught her, they caught her before me. And they caught-- you want to know this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught her, and they put her in the big prison in Paris. They put her on a board with nails sticking up. And they strapped her-- naked-- on that board. And they wanted her to admit all the people she knew. And every time she said no, they would bang her on it. I don't know. But her sister-- they arrested her sister-- who did nothing-- but just because she was her sister. And her sister said that they showed pictures. They showed her just to hurt her. They didn't arrest the sister-- just for a short time. Because the sister wasn't involved in anything. But they showed the sister pictures to make her feel bad, to try to make the sister say something. But she never did. And she was full of blood, just blood all over the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk some more about your experiences. You say you traveled in France, and you traveled to Belgium. Did you travel anywhere else for the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Just there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the group that you worked with, the underground group that you worked with?

speaker: interviewee
text: She never wanted to say it. Simone never said it. She didn't want me to know it. As I said, I started really with the underground, not knowing what I was doing, until the day she told me. I had started already. And she told me well, you know what you're doing? Do you mind, and this and that. And I said, no. And then things came so fast, and so fast, and so fast. Before we knew it, we were arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you started working for the underground sometime in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not '39. Yeah. After-- '40 I told you. I told you I knew her in '39. Then we were working to the fairs. Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about the months leading up to your arrest.

speaker: interviewee
text: About what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the months leading up to your arrest in 1941, by the Gestapo. Had things changed for you in terms of the work that you had done for the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well when-- you mean when I was arrested, or how they arrested me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that, leading up to when you were arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just still working at the Chamber of Commerce, until they arrested me, and going back and forth. And then one day, when they arrested me, they came. I had been weekend trout fishing with my future husband-- I was hoping-- and friends. And when I came back, I was sick. I had pleurisy. And I was in bed. And the owner of the building where I lived, came up and he said, she said, there's something funny going on. There are two Gestapo who want to speak to you. I think my heart went up in my throat. But I couldn't do anything. They were there. And they knocked at the door. And they came in. And they didn't ask if they could, they came in. And they told me [GERMAN]. Get up. I was in bed. And they start to go through all my-- all-- everything. They opened up all the drawers, all the closets, everything. They took everything they wanted, everything they could put their hands on. And I had, you know, little books with addresses and all. They took everything. They helped themselves to everything. And I was smoking at that time. I stopped a long time ago. And it was hard to get cigarettes. But John would always get me cigarettes, the best English cigarettes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They took everything. I can always see them. And I looked at them, and I thought, what are they taking that for, you know? They helped themselves to everything, everything. So they told me to get dressed. I'll always remember it. It was the style of the day. I had the Durban hats, which were very stylish, and an umbrella. They said come out. It was cold-- November. So they made me get dressed. They took me to the Gestapo in Brussels.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- I felt uncomfortable. I wasn't afraid, though. Anyway, they weren't talking to me. There was one sitting next to me, and one driving. And I could see he had a revolver there. And he was well protected. [CHUCKLES] He was protecting me. So anyway, we got to the Gestapo. And there the big [? chief ?] [? Baggins ?] was there. That's where I met him. And all night long, they questioned me, questioned me. And in back of me, like you're in front. But a while ago you were there. There's was an old guy there in back of me. Every time, they showed me pictures, and pictures, and pictures. And they'd ask me, [NON-ENGLISH]. You know them, you know them. And I'd tell them, no. Some I didn't know. Underground workers were [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't know. But some I did know. But I said, no. Every time I said no, they'd bang me on my head, every time. He had a great big-- like a bat, a flat bat-- knocked me on my head. And this [? Baggins ?], he was all so well dressed, and his nails were done and everything. And I was starved. And I was so thirsty. I had a fever. I had pleurisy. And I asked if I could have a glass of water. And he said, no. I couldn't have water. I couldn't talk anymore anyway. And he had great big silver trays come up with food. He did it on purpose. He knew I was hungry, probably, and thirsty. But I couldn't have anything. Anyway, they questioned me that first night. It must have been--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop the tape right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Enough?
